full_text:
[Dear Dr. Michele Hartsough,]{.mark}

[We are pleased to submit our revised manuscript, titled
**"**]{.mark}*Single-cell spatial atlas of high-grade serous ovarian
cancer uncovers MHC class II as a key predictor of spatial tumor
ecosystems and clinical outcomes*[**"**.]{.mark}

We sincerely thank the reviewers for their invaluable and positive
comments. We have now comprehensively addressed all the comments and
believe that the manuscript has been significantly strengthened.
[Importantly, we provide new experimental validations including **Xenium
single-cell spatial transcriptomics and **]{.mark}**functional ex vivo
experiments confirming the mechanistic role of MHCII in T-cell
activation.**

The key improvements in the manuscript include

1. Improved resolution of tumor-specific expression of MHCII - with
 > **Xenium single-cell transcriptomics, additional representations
 > of spatial proteomics, scRNAseq and imaging data analysis**
 > excluding the possibility of artefacts/spill over and
 > demonstrating true MHCII expression in the tumor cells.

2. Additional compelling evidence supporting the role of tumor MHCII in
 > HGSC taking into account **tumor genomic features, anatomical site
 > variation and associated immune cell signatures.**

3. Additional validation on the effect of tumor MHCII independently of
 > MHCI demonstrating MHCII specific effect on CD8+ T-cell tumor

n and associated immune cell signatures.**

3. Additional validation on the effect of tumor MHCII independently of
 > MHCI demonstrating MHCII specific effect on CD8+ T-cell tumor
 > reactivity.

4. New analysis demonstrating the **technical robustness of CEFIIRA**,
 > excluding the effect of overall feature variability explaining the
 > model performance.

5. Improved malignant-compartment specific immunopeptidomic analysis
 > showing **MHCII antigens linked to cytoskeleton and adhesion**
 > providing the mechanistic background of MHCII expression on tumor
 > cells.

6. **New functional ex vivo experiments confirming the mechanistic role
 > of MHCII in T-cell activation**. Using our novel platform of
 > patient-derived immune-competent cultures (iPDCs) we demonstrate
 > that MHCII blockade abolishes T-cell activation and cytokine
 > production. These findings underscore the mechanistic dependency
 > of T-cell activation on MHCII, offering valuable insights for
 > optimizing immunotherapy strategies and improving patient outcomes
 > in cancer treatment

**The key findings of our manuscript are summarized below:**

1. [Direct spatial link between MHCII⁺ tumor cells and immune
 > hotspots.]{.underline} We demonstrate, for the first time, that
 > intra-tumoral immune hotspots spatially coincide with **MHCII
 > expression on cancer cells**. Adjacent immune cells are **more

erline} We demonstrate, for the first time, that
 > intra-tumoral immune hotspots spatially coincide with **MHCII
 > expression on cancer cells**. Adjacent immune cells are **more
 > activated and tumor-reactive**, establishing MHCII as a driver of
 > localized immune activation.

2. [Immune activation is spatially confined.]{.underline} The
 > composition of the tumor--stroma boundary shapes immune activity
 > **independently of tumor molecular features**.These
 > microanatomical structures influence local immune behavior in ways
 > not detectable by bulk or non-spatial methods.

3. [Mechanistic validation of MHCII as a predictive immunotherapy
 > biomarker.]{.underline} Tumors with high MHCII expression show
 > **enhanced responsiveness to immune checkpoint blockade**.
 > Blocking MHCII function eliminates this benefit, confirming a
 > **causal role** in treatment response.

[Altogether, our]{.mark} work establishes a mechanistic, spatially
resolved link between tumor-intrinsic MHCII expression and localized
immune activation, defines how microanatomical interfaces shape immune
behavior, and demonstrates that MHCII function predicts response to
immune checkpoint blockade. Our [findings now have significant clinical
implications, suggesting that MHC-II expression predicts overall tumor
immunogenicity, potentially stratifying patients who benefit from PD-1

indings now have significant clinical
implications, suggesting that MHC-II expression predicts overall tumor
immunogenicity, potentially stratifying patients who benefit from PD-1
inhibition, and guide the development of alternative therapeutic
approaches for MHC-II--negative HGSCs.]{.mark}

[Thank you for considering our manuscript for publication. We look
forward to the opportunity to contribute to *Cancer Discovery*.]{.mark}

[Yours sincerely,]{.mark}
